featuredSetups.label: Growth to a target
featuredSetups.description: Open a clean growth case with the target marker, opposite-rate curve, and log guide all visible.

setup.note: Use this when you want the target hit time and the logarithm step to stay attached to the same curve.

featuredSetups.label: Decay and half-life
featuredSetups.description: Start from a decay case and keep the half-life guide visible while the target sits below the start.

setup.note: This keeps the falling curve, the half-life cue, and the inverse-time readout on one bench.

pageIntro.definition: Use one live exponential curve to connect growth, decay, doubling or half-life, and the logarithm that answers a target-time question.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: In y(t) = y_0 e^{kt}, the sign of k decides growth or decay, repeated multiplication sets the doubling/half-life cadence, and ln(T / y_0) solves the target-time question.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: It keeps logarithms attached to a visible crossing on the graph, so inverse-time work feels like reading the same curve rather than switching chapters.

paragraphs: Exponential change is easiest to trust when the growth or decay stays attached to one live curve instead of a detached rule sheet. This bench keeps the starting value, the continuous rate, the target line, and the inverse-time question tied to the same graph.

The key move is multiplicative rather than additive. Equal steps in time multiply the amount by the same factor, which is why growth can double and decay can halve on a fixed schedule. The logarithm appears only when you turn the question around and ask how long it takes to reach a chosen target.

keyIdeas: In an exponential model, the amount follows y(t) = y_0 e^{kt}, so the sign of k decides whether the curve grows or decays.
Doubling time and half-life are fixed time scales because exponential change repeats by multiplication, not by adding the same amount each step.
The inverse question is logarithmic: solving for time means taking ln(target / y_0) and dividing by the rate.

commonMisconception.myth: Exponential change means the graph always rises quickly, and logarithms are a separate chapter with no direct connection to the curve.
correction: A negative rate gives honest exponential decay, so the same model can fall toward zero instead of rising away from the start.
The logarithm is what appears when you solve the exponential target equation for time. It is the inverse question, not a disconnected new object.

workedExamples.title: Live worked examples
workedExamples.intro: This concept keeps the bench compact, so the main curve, the target line, the cadence readout, and the log view carry the core reasoning directly.

items.title: Target time from the ratio
items.prompt: A population starts at 3 units and grows at a continuous rate of 0.25 until it reaches 12 units. How long should that take?

steps.label: 1. Compare the target to the start
steps.template: The target is four times the starting value, so \(T / y_0 = 12 / 3 = 4\).

steps.label: 3. Check the graph against the cadence
steps.template: Because \(\ln 4 = 2 \ln 2\), this target sits at exactly two doubling times, so the hit marker should land a little past 5.5 time units.

items.resultTemplate: The curve reaches the target after about 5.55 time units.
items.interpretationTemplate: This keeps the logarithm tied to one visible crossing: the growth curve must double twice to turn 3 into 12.

miniChallenge.prompt: Set a decay case where the target is below the start and the target time is longer than one half-life.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the target should sit just below the start or much lower before you test it.
miniChallenge.answer: The target must sit well below the starting value so the curve needs more than one half-life before it reaches it.
miniChallenge.explanation: One half-life only cuts the amount in half. If the target is much lower than that first halfway mark, the inverse-time readout has to land later than one half-life.

equations.label: Exponential change rule
equations.meaning: Uses one starting value and one continuous rate to generate exponential growth or decay.

equations.label: Target time from a logarithm
equations.meaning: Solves the inverse question by turning the multiplicative target ratio into a logarithm.

equations.label: Fixed exponential time scales
equations.meaning: Shows the fixed time scale for doubling in growth and halving in decay.

variableLinks.label: Initial value
variableLinks.description: Sets the starting height of both the current curve and the opposite-rate comparison curve.

variableLinks.label: Rate
variableLinks.description: Changes whether the curve grows or decays and sets the doubling time, half-life, and inverse target time together.

variableLinks.label: Target
variableLinks.description: Moves the chosen target so the hit marker and the logarithmic inverse-time line both update.

controls.label: Initial value
controls.description: Set the starting height of the exponential curve.

controls.label: Rate
controls.description: Use positive values for growth and negative values for decay.

controls.label: Target
controls.description: Choose the amount you want the curve to reach.

presets.label: Growth to target
presets.description: A clean growth case where the target sits above the start and is reached inside the window.

presets.label: Decay half-life
presets.description: A decay case where the target is below the start and the half-life readout matters immediately.

presets.label: Slow growth
presets.description: A smaller positive rate keeps the target time longer even though the curve still rises.

presets.label: Unreachable target
presets.description: A decay case with a target above the start so the inverse-time question has no nonnegative answer.

overlays.label: Target marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the target line and any reachable hit point on the main curve.
whatToNotice: The hit point only appears when the target sits on the side of the start that matches the sign of the rate.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the inverse question tied to one visible crossing instead of a detached equation.

overlays.label: Opposite-rate comparison
overlays.shortDescription: Show the same start value with the opposite sign of the rate.
whatToNotice: The paired curve answers the growth-versus-decay comparison without changing the start value.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the sign of the rate visible on the same bench instead of treating growth and decay as different chapters.

overlays.label: Doubling or half-life guide
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the current doubling time or half-life and the matching amount on the main graph when one exists.
whatToNotice: A fixed multiplicative time scale appears only when the rate is meaningfully positive or negative.
The guide lands on the amount reached after one full doubling or one full half-life step.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns doubling time and half-life into visible bench features instead of slogans.

overlays.label: Log guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the target-log line that the straightened log view must meet.
whatToNotice: The log view turns the target question into one straight-line crossing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the cleanest way to see why the inverse question is logarithmic.

graphs.label: Amount vs time
graphs.description: Shows the current exponential curve, the opposite-rate comparison, and the target marker on the same amount-versus-time graph.

graphs.label: Log target view
graphs.description: Shows how the logarithm straightens the target question into one line crossing.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts when the target-time algebra starts to drift away from the curve.

items.text: Switch the sign of the rate and keep the same start value. One curve rises, the other falls, and the target only stays reachable on one side of the start.
The log view turns the curved target question into one straight-line crossing because $\ln(y / y_0) = k t$.
When the rate is positive, the same fixed time keeps doubling the amount. When the rate is negative, the matching fixed time keeps halving it.
Try the unreachable-target preset and ask which side of the start the target sits on before you touch the logarithm.
items.tryThis: Apply the Unreachable target preset, then compare the target line with the current decay direction before reading the inverse-time card.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Commit to the graph change before you apply it.

items.prompt: If the rate changes from positive to negative while the start and target stay fixed above the start value, what should happen first?
items.scenarioLabel: Flip the sign of the rate

choices.label: The curve turns into decay and the higher target is no longer reached.
The curve keeps growing, but the logarithm disappears.
Only the target line moves while the curve stays the same.

items.explanation: Changing only the sign of k flips growth into decay, so a target above the start stops being reachable on this bench.
items.observationHint: Watch the target marker and the opposite-rate comparison together.

items.prompt: In a growth case, if the target is pushed farther above the start while the rate stays fixed, what happens to the target time?

choices.label: The target time increases because the same rate has farther to go multiplicatively.
The target time decreases because the target line is higher.
The target time stays exactly the same because k did not change.

items.explanation: With the same positive rate, a larger target ratio means a larger logarithm and therefore a larger hit time.
items.observationHint: Compare the target-log line before and after the target changes.

items.prompt: If the positive rate doubles while the start and target stay fixed, what should happen to the doubling time?

choices.label: The doubling time becomes shorter.
The doubling time becomes longer.
The doubling time stays fixed because doubling only depends on the start value.

items.explanation: Doubling time is $\ln 2 / k$, so a larger positive k makes that fixed time smaller.
items.observationHint: Watch the cadence guide move left when the rate grows.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live curve and the inverse-time readout together.

questions.prompt: Which single change turns the same exponential bench from growth into decay?

choices.label: Make the rate k negative.
Raise the target while keeping k positive.
Lower the initial value while keeping k positive.

questions.explanation: The sign of k decides whether the amount grows or decays. Changing the target or the start value does not flip the direction of change.

showMeAction.label: Show a decay case
showMeAction.observationHint: The same bench now falls toward zero because k is negative.

questions.prompt: A decay curve starts at 5 while the target is 12. What should the inverse-time readout say?

choices.label: The target is not reached for the current sign of k.
The target time must be negative, so the curve reaches it faster.
The target time stays equal to the half-life no matter where the target sits.

questions.explanation: A decay curve can only move downward from its positive start value, so a higher target sits on the wrong side of the graph for a nonnegative hit time.

showMeAction.label: Show the unreachable target
showMeAction.observationHint: The target line sits above the start while the curve keeps falling away from it.

questions.prompt: Why does a logarithm appear when you solve the target equation for time?

choices.label: Because time sits in the exponent, so solving for it means undoing an exponential with a logarithm.
Because every curved graph automatically turns into a logarithm.
Because logarithms are only used to rename the initial value.

questions.explanation: The logarithm is the inverse step that unwraps the exponential when time is trapped in the exponent.

showMeAction.label: Show the log guide
showMeAction.observationHint: The straightened log view makes the inverse step visible as one line crossing.

questions.prompt: What is the cleanest difference between doubling time and half-life on this bench?

choices.label: Doubling time belongs to growth cases, while half-life belongs to decay cases.
They are the same only because the target line is hidden.
They change the initial value instead of describing equal-time multiplicative change.

questions.explanation: Both are equal-time multiplicative benchmarks, but one describes repeated doubling and the other repeated halving.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: The target line does not decide whether the case is doubling or halving.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The initial value can stay fixed while the benchmark still describes the time-based multiplicative change.

questions.prompt: When you solve for a target time, which quantity matters first?

choices.label: The target-to-start ratio, because the curve changes multiplicatively.
Only the difference target minus start, because exponentials behave additively.
Only the graph color, because the inverse readout ignores the current curve.

questions.explanation: The inverse-time question starts from how many times larger or smaller the target is than the start value.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Exponential change is multiplicative, so the ratio matters more than a raw difference.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The inverse readout is tied to the live curve and its current target ratio.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the curve, target line, and half-life cue together. The goal is to read a decay target as a multiplicative story, not just to land one number by accident.

items.title: Quarter-target checkpoint
items.prompt: Build a decay case where the target is about one quarter of the start, so the curve reaches it in about two half-lives.
items.successMessage: You built a decay target that reads as two honest half-life steps. The ratio, the half-life cue, and the inverse target time are now all telling the same growth-versus-decay story.

setup.note: Start from a simple decay case, then lower the target and sharpen the decay until the target-time readout matches two half-life steps.

hints: A quarter-target means the target ratio should be close to $0.25$, not $0.5$.
Two half-lives means the hit time should be about twice the half-life shown on the bench.

targets.label: Keep the start value near $8$, between $7.9$ and $8.1$.
Keep the continuous decay rate between $-0.24$ and $-0.22$.
Set the target near one quarter of the start, between $1.95$ and $2.05$.

paragraphs: The simulation shows an exponential amount-versus-time curve with a starting value, a target line, an opposite-rate comparison curve, a doubling-time or half-life guide that lands on the matching amount, and a smaller log view that straightens the inverse target question.

One graph shows the current exponential curve, the opposite-rate comparison, the target crossing, and the one-step doubling or half-life amount when that cue exists. A second graph shows ln(amount / initial) as a straight line with the matching target-log line.

seo.title: Exponential Change / Growth, Decay, and Logarithms
seo.description: Explore exponential growth and decay with one live curve, a target marker, doubling time or half-life readouts, and a log view that answers how long it takes to reach a target.

v2.intuition: Exponential change becomes one story when the same curve shows direction, repeated multiplication, and the time needed to hit a target.
v2.whyItMatters: This links growth/decay, doubling/half-life, and logarithms before learners meet them as separate formula drills.

equationSnapshot.note: Read the model first as a live curve, then use the log equation only when the question is turned around to solve for time.

guidedSteps.title: Read one growth case
guidedSteps.goal: Tie the starting value, positive rate, target line, and doubling guide to one curve.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start from Growth to target, keep Amount vs time open, and move Target while the target marker and doubling guide stay visible.
guidedSteps.notice: The curve reaches higher targets later, but one doubling step still marks the same multiplicative cadence.
guidedSteps.explain: A positive rate multiplies the amount by the same factor over equal time intervals, so the target crossing depends on the target-to-start ratio.

guidedSteps.title: Flip growth to decay
guidedSteps.goal: Use the sign of k to decide whether the amount rises or falls and whether the target is reachable.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch to Decay half-life, then compare the falling curve with the opposite-rate comparison on the same start value.
guidedSteps.notice: The half-life guide replaces the doubling guide, and a target above the start is now on the wrong side of the motion.
guidedSteps.explain: Growth and decay are not separate rules here. They are the same exponential model with opposite signs for k.

guidedSteps.title: Solve time with logs
guidedSteps.goal: Connect the logarithm to the inverse question: when does this curve hit that target?
guidedSteps.doThis: Return to Growth to target, open the Log target view, and raise the target while the log guide stays on.
guidedSteps.notice: The curved amount graph and the straight log view point to the same target-hit time.
guidedSteps.explain: Because time is in the exponent, solving y_0 e^{kt} = T means taking ln(T / y_0) before dividing by k.

guidedSteps.title: Check cadence vs target
guidedSteps.goal: Explain a target hit using doubling time or half-life instead of only plugging into the formula.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use the quick checks, then try the challenge: build a decay case where a quarter-target takes about two half-lives.
guidedSteps.notice: A quarter of the start is two halving steps, so the target-hit time should line up with about two half-life intervals.
guidedSteps.explain: Strong exponential reasoning keeps the ratio, the graph crossing, and the fixed multiplicative time scale in agreement.

learned: The sign of k turns the same exponential model into growth or decay.
Equal time steps multiply the amount, which creates fixed doubling-time or half-life cadences.
Target-time questions are logarithmic because they ask when the exponential curve reaches a chosen ratio.
A graph crossing, a log-view crossing, and a doubling/half-life readout should tell the same story.
wrapUp.misconception: Do not treat logarithms as a detached new topic here. The logarithm is the inverse-time question for the same exponential curve.

nextConcepts.title: Derivative as Slope / Local Rate of Change
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry changing steepness into local slope on the graph

nextConcepts.title: Radioactivity and Half-Life
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: See the same decay law inside a physics bench with real half-life language

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Build one growth case and one decay case where the target-hit time can be explained from the ratio before you read the formula.
